back: A functional dependency X → Y holds in a relation R if for every tuple t1, t2 in R, whenever t1[X] = t2[X], then t1[Y] = t2[Y]. In simple terms, X determines Y.
A functional dependency X → Y is trivial if Y ⊆ X (i.e., Y is a subset of X). Example: AB → A, AB → B are trivial FDs.
A functional dependency X → Y is non-trivial if Y ⊄ X (Y is not a subset of X). Example: StudentID → StudentName (non-trivial).
A functional dependency X → Y is completely non-trivial if X ∩ Y = ∅ (X and Y share no common attributes). Example: EmpID → DeptName (completely non-trivial).
The closure of attribute set X (denoted X+) is the set of all attributes that can be functionally determined from X using Armstrong's axioms.

1. Reflexivity: If Y ⊆ X, then X → Y
2. Augmentation: If X → Y, then XZ → YZ
3. Transitivity: If X → Y and Y → Z, then X → Z
A superkey is a set of attributes SK in relation R such that for every possible relation r(R), no two tuples have the same SK values. Formally: SK → ALL ATTRIBUTES of R.
A candidate key is a minimal superkey - a superkey with no proper subset that is also a superkey. There can be multiple candidate keys in a relation.
The minimal cover (or minimal basis) of a set of FDs F is a minimal set of FDs equivalent to F where: no FD contains extraneous attributes, and no FD can be derived from others.
A canonical cover is a minimal set of FDs where each FD has a single attribute on the right-hand side (Y is singleton) and no extraneous attributes on the left.
A decomposition of R into R1 and R2 is lossless (lossless-join) if (R1 ∩ R2) → R1 or (R1 ∩ R2) → R2 holds in the closure of F. It ensures no information is lost during join.
A decomposition preserves dependencies if the closure of the union of projections of F on each Ri equals the closure of F. i.e., (F1 ∪ F2 ∪ ...)+ = F+.

Union: If X→Y and X→Z, then X→YZ
Decomposition: If X→YZ, then X→Y and X→Z
Pseudotransitive: If X→Y and WY→Z, then WX→Z
A prime attribute is an attribute that appears in any candidate key of relation R. Non-prime attributes do not appear in any candidate key.
An attribute A in a functional dependency X → Y is extraneous if X → Y is logically implied by the set of FDs without A (i.e., (X - {A})+ contains Y).
